1: Date: sixth year, sixth month, fifth day (592 BCE). Ezekiel is sitting with the elders of Judah — the exilic leadership consulting the prophet — when the vision begins. The hand of YHWH seizing the prophet while he is in a social setting (elders sitting with him) marks the involuntary nature of the prophetic experience. Christ's ministry similarly breaks into ordinary social settings — meals, synagogues, journeys — bringing the divine encounter into the mundane moment.
2: 'I looked, and there was a figure that looked like a man. Downward from what looked like his waist was fire, and from his waist upward he looked like glowing metal, as bright as fire' — the same figure as in Ezekiel 1:27, now appearing in the temple vision. This figure of fire and glowing metal is the divine messenger who will guide Ezekiel through the temple. The resurrected Christ appears in similar terms in Revelation 1:14-15 (eyes like a flame of fire, feet like burnished bronze, voice like many waters). The figure of judgment in Ezekiel 8 anticipates the figure of the risen Christ who walks among the seven lampstands.
3: 'He stretched out what looked like a hand and took me by the hair of my head; the Spirit lifted me up between heaven and earth and brought me in visions of God to Jerusalem, to the north gate of the inner court, where the idol that provokes jealousy stood' — the Spirit's transportation of the prophet to the temple to see its corruption. Jesus, transported in his temptation to the 'pinnacle of the temple' (Matthew 4:5), sees the same site of religious corruption that Ezekiel witnesses in vision. The 'idol that provokes to jealousy' (semel haqin'ah) set at the north gate of the inner court is exactly what the temple mount had become under Manasseh (2 Kings 21:7).
4: 'The glory of the God of Israel was there, like the vision I had seen in the valley' — the contrast at the heart of the vision: the glory of YHWH remains present in the temple even as the abominations accumulate around it. Before the glory departs (Ezekiel 10), it is still there, witnessing the defilement. Christ similarly remains present in the temple of Jerusalem, overturning tables and teaching (Matthew 21:12-17), the divine presence refusing to leave without being heard.
5: 'Son of man, look toward the north' — the first of four abominations, all located in different parts of the temple precinct. The systematic tour of the temple's four corruption sites prefigures Jesus's comprehensive indictment of the temple establishment in Matt 23 (woes against scribes and Pharisees) and his announcement of the temple's destruction (Matthew 24:2). Both the prophet's vision-tour and the Messiah's physical tour arrive at the same verdict: the house has become desolate (Matthew 23:38).
6: 'Son of man, do you see what they are doing — the utterly detestable practices the house of Israel is committing here, that will drive me far from my sanctuary?' — the abominations driving YHWH away. The divine presence is not compelled to remain where it is dishonored. The same logic governs the departure of the Holy Spirit's influence from a community that grieves him (Ephesians 4:30; 1 Thessalonians 5:19). The exile of the glory precedes the exile of the people; the Spirit's withdrawal precedes spiritual death.
7: 'He brought me to the entrance of the court, and I looked and saw a hole in the wall' — the hidden entrance to the inner corruption. The hole in the wall that Ezekiel digs through at God's command reveals what is being done in secret. Jesus applies the same principle: 'there is nothing hidden that will not be disclosed, and nothing concealed that will not be known or brought out into the open' (Luke 8:17). The secret idolatry of Ezekiel's temple points to the judgment-day disclosure of all hidden things.
8: 'Son of man, dig into the wall' — Ezekiel is commanded to be the agent of discovery, digging through to see the hidden corruption. The act anticipates the gospel's role as the revealer of what has been concealed — Christ as the one who brings hidden things to light (1 Corinthians 4:5; Ephesians 5:13). The digging through the wall is the action that prepares the vision: revelation requires the removal of the obscuring surface.
9: 'Go in and see the wicked and detestable things they are doing here' — YHWH's invitation to see the full reality of the sin that provokes judgment. The divine willingness to show his prophet the full extent of the corruption corresponds to the NT's unflinching diagnosis of sin: 'all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God' (Romans 3:23). The vision of sin's full extent is necessary for the appreciation of grace's full sufficiency.
10: 'I went in and looked, and I saw portrayed all over the walls all kinds of crawling things and unclean animals and all the idols of Israel' — the walls covered with images of unclean animals and idols. The contrast with the cherubim-and-palm decorative program of Ezekiel's ideal temple (Ezekiel 41:18-19) is total. Where the true temple will be covered with images of divine messengers and life-giving trees, the corrupted temple is covered with images of unclean animals and false gods. The decorative program reveals the theological program.
11: 'Seventy of the elders of Israel were standing there, with Jaazaniah son of Shaphan standing among them. Each had a censer in his hand, and the fragrant incense cloud was rising' — seventy elders (the number of the Sanhedrin, the full representative body of Israel) officiating at the illicit worship. The incense rising to the images is the most blatant possible appropriation of the worship that belongs to YHWH. The number seventy and the censer-wielding echoes Numbers 11:16-25 (the seventy elders who received the Spirit); the spiritual leadership has exchanged Spirit-empowered service for idolatrous imitation.
12: 'Son of man, have you seen what the elders of Israel are doing in the darkness, each in his chamber of images? They say, The LORD does not see us; the LORD has forsaken the land' — the theology that enables secret sin: 'the LORD does not see.' Jesus addresses this directly in the parable of the wicked servants (Luke 12:45: 'my master is delayed') — the belief that divine absence enables sin. Paul's response: 'Nothing in all creation is hidden from God's sight' (Hebrews 4:13). The One who sees in secret rewards in secret and also judges in secret.
13: 'You will see them doing things that are even more detestable' — the escalating tour of corruption: each abomination worse than the last. The progression of spiritual decline has no natural floor — without the intervention of grace, corruption deepens. The gospel interrupts the escalation: 'where sin increased, grace increased all the more' (Romans 5:20). The downward spiral of Ezekiel's temple tour is answered by the upward spiral of Romans 5-8.
14: 'He brought me to the entrance of the north gate of the LORD's house, and I saw women sitting there, weeping for Tammuz' — women weeping for the Babylonian vegetation deity Tammuz (Dumuzi), whose annual death and descent to the underworld was mourned in a major Babylonian religious festival. The women's tears for Tammuz — a deity whose death and (possible) resurrection was the center of a pagan cult — stand in ironic contrast with the tears that belong to Christ's death: 'Do not weep for me; weep for yourselves and for your children' (Luke 23:28). The tears of the women at the cross (Luke 23:27) are the true fulfillment of the tears that pagan religion misdirected toward Tammuz.
15: 'Do you see this? You will see things even more detestable than these' — the repeated escalation formula. The progressive revelation of greater and greater corruption prefigures the process of conviction by the Holy Spirit (John 16:8-11) — the Spirit who brings the full extent of sin to light before the full extent of grace can be received. YHWH showing Ezekiel things 'even more detestable' is the uncomfortable work of thorough conviction.
16: 'He brought me into the inner court of the LORD's house, and there at the entrance to the temple, between the porch and the altar, were about twenty-five men. With their backs toward the temple of the LORD and their faces toward the east, they were bowing down to the sun in the east' — the most egregious abomination: the priests facing away from the Holy of Holies to worship the sun. The posture — backs to YHWH, faces to the sun-god — is the visual expression of apostasy. Christ calls for the reversal: 'turn from darkness to light' (Acts 26:18). The men facing east to worship the creation are the type of those who 'exchanged the truth about God for a lie and worshiped and served created things rather than the Creator' (Romans 1:25).
17: 'Have you seen this, son of man? Is it a trivial matter for the house of Judah to do the detestable things they are doing here? Must they also fill the land with violence and continually provoke me to anger? Look at them putting the branch to their nose!' — the 'branch to the nose' (sholeychim et-hazmorah el-appam) is an obscure gesture, possibly a pagan ritual act of contempt or an idolatrous practice. The connection between idolatry and violence is consistent throughout the prophets: false worship of other gods produces social violence and injustice. Christ's reversal addresses both simultaneously — 'love the Lord your God' (the vertical) and 'love your neighbor as yourself' (the horizontal) as the two commandments on which everything hangs (Matthew 22:37-40).
18: 'Therefore I will act in wrath; my eye will not spare them, nor will I have pity. Although they shout in my ears, I will not listen to them' — the divine refusal to hear prayer from those who continue in willful idolatry. Psalms 66:18 states the same principle: 'if I had cherished sin in my heart, the Lord would not have listened.' Christ makes the same demand: the one who hears his words and does not do them is like the foolish builder (Matthew 7:26-27). Yet even in this 'will not listen,' Christ has become the mediator who makes prayer effective: the one prayer YHWH always hears is the cry of the one in Christ (Hebrews 7:25; Romans 8:34).
